title: Tales From The Public Domain

jokes.text: Overdue book? This is the biggest frame-up since O.J.
jokes.setup: Homer gets a library notice for an overdue book
jokes.punchline: Compares minor library fine to the O.J. Simpson trial
jokes.explanation: Strong character comedy - Homer's tendency to see persecution everywhere. Good cultural reference with surprising connection.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. Blood in the Bronco, the cuts on his hands, those Jay Leno monologues! Oh, my God! He did it!
jokes.punchline: Lists actual evidence alongside Jay Leno jokes as equally damning
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the previous joke with absurd logic that Jay Leno monologues are evidence of guilt.

jokes.text: I checked that out when Bart was born. I was gonna read to him every day. What happened? Stuff kept coming up. Mostly car-related. Piece of crap.
jokes.setup: Homer had noble intentions to read to baby Bart
jokes.punchline: Admits he never followed through, blames it on cars, then insults the car
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer characterization - starts with good intentions, reveals laziness, then gets distracted by car complaints.

jokes.text: I decide who reads and when!
jokes.setup: Family suggests Homer read the book now
jokes.punchline: Homer asserts arbitrary authority over reading
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer power trip over something meaningless. Good economy and character consistency.

jokes.text: Homer's Odyssey. Is this about that minivan I rented once?
jokes.punchline: Thinks it's literally about his personal vehicle experience
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay on Homer's name plus perfect character obliviousness. Efficient setup and payoff.

jokes.text: That minivan had the biggest cup holders. And change slots for every coin, from penny to quarter.
jokes.setup: Homer continues fixating on the minivan
jokes.punchline: Gets obsessively detailed about mundane car features
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency showing Homer's ability to get lost in irrelevant details.

jokes.text: Dad, I loved it, too, but it was seven years ago.
jokes.setup: Homer won't stop talking about the minivan
jokes.punchline: Lisa reveals how pathetically long Homer has been obsessing over this rental
jokes.explanation: Lisa as the voice of reason, revealing the absurd duration of Homer's fixation.

jokes.text: Hi, odiddily-Odysseus!
jokes.setup: Ned Flanders appears as a character in the Odyssey
jokes.punchline: Uses his signature speech pattern with the Greek hero's name
jokes.explanation: Perfect integration of Flanders' characteristic speech into ancient Greek setting. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I think I speak for all the Greeks when I say this war has gone on for too long. I'll say. I'd really like to go out and get the mail.
jokes.setup: Odysseus makes grand statement about the lengthy war
jokes.punchline: Flanders has mundane domestic concerns about checking mail
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between epic concerns and suburban banality. Flanders character stays consistent.

jokes.text: Anyway, over torture, one of your soldiers mentioned that you collect giant wooden animals. We hope you don't have a horse. Well, I don't have one from you. Bring it in!
jokes.setup: Odysseus casually mentions torture and offers a horse gift
jokes.punchline: Trojan king enthusiastically accepts despite the obvious setup
jokes.explanation: Plays on the famous story with modern casual tone. The torture mention is darkly funny.

jokes.text: Now throughout history, when people get wood, they'll think of Trojans.
jokes.punchline: Double entendre referencing both lumber and condoms
jokes.explanation: Brilliant double entendre that works on multiple levels - historical, sexual, and brand reference. Very quotable.

jokes.text: What are you laughing at, Dad? If I'm laughing at what I think I am, it's very funny.
jokes.setup: Lisa notices Homer laughing at the double entendre
jokes.punchline: Homer acknowledges the adult humor without explaining it
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor acknowledging the adult joke while maintaining the family show format.

jokes.text: Aw, look at them. Sleeping like little angels.
jokes.setup: Greeks about to massacre sleeping Trojans
jokes.punchline: Odysseus finds them adorable right before ordering their slaughter
jokes.explanation: Dark humor contrasting peaceful imagery with imminent violence. Good structure with innocent setup.

jokes.text: Remember, just 'cause their heads are off, it don't mean they're dead.
jokes.punchline: Absurdly practical advice about decapitation not guaranteeing death
jokes.explanation: Absurd practical advice that sounds like Homer's workplace safety tips applied to warfare.

jokes.text: Forget it! Sacrificing animals is barbaric. Now have the slaves kill the wounded.
jokes.setup: Odysseus refuses animal sacrifice as barbaric
jokes.punchline: Immediately orders human execution as if it's more civilized
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark irony highlighting moral inconsistency. Sharp structure with immediate contradiction.

jokes.text: You fat lush. You just destroyed Atlantis. You used to be fun.
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions destroying Atlantis as collateral damage, calls him unfun
jokes.explanation: Gods portrayed as bickering family members, with major historical destruction treated casually.

jokes.text: Where's the Zeus who used to turn into a cow and pick up chicks? He grew up. Maybe you should, too.
jokes.setup: Poseidon reminiscing about Zeus's wild days
jokes.punchline: Double meaning of 'chicks' as both baby birds and women
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay referencing Zeus's mythological shapeshifting with modern dating slang.

jokes.text: Greece is the word.
jokes.punchline: Parody of 'Grease is the word' from the musical
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective pun on a well-known song. Low effort but recognizable.

jokes.text: Is it 'vase' or 'vase'? You gonna be asking that the whole trip?
jokes.punchline: Mundane pronunciation debate during mythological adventure
jokes.explanation: Contrast between epic setting and petty domestic concerns. Moderately funny.

jokes.text: Gentlemen, I must be wasted, 'cause it looks like that cloud is mad at me.
jokes.setup: Odysseus notices something odd about a cloud
jokes.punchline: Assumes he must be drunk rather than considering divine intervention
jokes.explanation: Good character logic - Homer would blame drinking before considering supernatural causes.

jokes.text: On the island Island of Sirens Our hot sex Will leave you perspiring Hey, that's kind of catchy.
jokes.punchline: Crude and obvious lyrics that Odysseus finds catchy anyway
jokes.explanation: Parody of mythical siren songs with deliberately bad modern lyrics. Homer's simple approval fits character.

jokes.text: It's coming from that island. Let's steer heedlessly towards it. Heedlessly it is.
jokes.setup: Hearing the siren song from an island
jokes.punchline: Uses 'heedlessly' properly while doing exactly what they shouldn't
jokes.explanation: Characters knowingly making the wrong choice with formal language. Good awareness of their own poor judgment.

jokes.text: Boy, if they kiss as good as they lure, wow! On the island We'll sex you up
jokes.setup: Odysseus expressing attraction to the sirens
jokes.punchline: More crude lyrics that parody Color Me Badd
jokes.explanation: Continuation of crude siren song parody. Reference to '90s song adds some cultural specificity.

jokes.text: Oh, God! They're hideous! Someone gouge my eyes! Save me from the ladies!
jokes.punchline: They're actually ugly, subverting the mythological expectation
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of mythological expectations. Visual punchline that works even in transcript form.

jokes.text: Discus Stu has ouzo for twozo.
jokes.setup: Disco Stu as a suitor for Penelope
jokes.punchline: Maintains his rhyming speech pattern in ancient Greek setting
jokes.explanation: Perfect character integration - Disco Stu's signature style adapted to Greek setting. Very quotable and efficient.

jokes.text: I'll leave you guys alone. Discus Stu was talking to you.
jokes.setup: Penelope tries to leave awkward situation
jokes.punchline: Disco Stu clarifies he was hitting on her specifically
jokes.explanation: Awkward pickup scene with good character consistency. Stu's persistence is appropriately cringey.

jokes.text: Ain't I a stinker?
jokes.punchline: Bugs Bunny's catchphrase in ancient Greek setting
jokes.explanation: Random pop culture insertion that doesn't integrate well with the story. Feels like cheap referential humor.

jokes.text: Boy, who decided to give every weirdo an island?
jokes.setup: Arriving at yet another magical island
jokes.punchline: Modern complaint about mythological island distribution
jokes.explanation: Good modern perspective on mythological tropes. Homer's practical annoyance works well.

jokes.text: Well, I was in the mood for something bubbling. Out of the way, slim.
jokes.setup: Offered a drink from a witch's cauldron
jokes.punchline: Homer's enthusiasm for any beverage overrides caution, calls sorceress 'slim'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer behavior - appetite overcoming survival instincts. The casual 'slim' adds character.

jokes.text: That pig looks like Lenny. Hey! Mmm. That's the next best thing to eating Lenny.
jokes.punchline: Considers it a cooking upgrade rather than a tragedy
jokes.explanation: Perfectly dark Homer logic - friend's transformation seen as culinary opportunity. Great character consistency.

jokes.text: Those were my friends? Yes. I've been saying that for hours. That's it! I'm going home!
jokes.setup: Homer slowly realizes he ate his transformed friends
jokes.punchline: This is what finally motivates him to leave, not the witchcraft
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Homer's priorities - cannibalism is his breaking point, not magic or danger.

jokes.text: You must go through Hades, crossing the River Styx. You're my lady Of the morning
jokes.punchline: River Styx becomes song lyric about Styx band
jokes.explanation: Rock band pun on mythological river name. Somewhat forced but recognizable reference.

jokes.text: Oh, this truly is hell!
jokes.setup: Hearing bad music in the underworld
jokes.punchline: The music confirms he's in hell more than the location
jokes.explanation: Efficient reaction joke tying bad music to hellish experience.

jokes.text: When we came here, Helen of Troy was hot! Now look at her! This is the face that launched a thousand ships, the other way.
jokes.setup: Complaining about how long they've been waiting
jokes.punchline: Helen's beauty has declined so much she'd repel ships instead
jokes.explanation: Excellent inversion of the famous 'face that launched a thousand ships' phrase. Strong structure and payoff.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm sorry I was gone so long, but I'm gonna do something I haven't done for 20 years. Take out the trash!
jokes.punchline: Homer wants to do household chores, then violence
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - sets up romantic expectation then subverts with domestic then violent reality.

jokes.text: Sweet Penelope, you're just as beautiful as you were when I left. Oh, I can't stay mad at you.
jokes.punchline: Penelope's response suggests she was actually angry at him
jokes.explanation: Good relationship dynamics showing Marge's typical long-suffering patience with Homer.

jokes.text: Brave Odysseus, it's been 20 long years. Regale me with tales of your adventures. Quit suffocating me. I'm going to Moe's.
jokes.setup: Wife wants to hear about epic adventures
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately escapes to the bar
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer characterization - epic hero who just wants to drink. Great subversion of reunion expectations.

jokes.text: Ooh! Did you say Joan Van Ark? No, Mom. Joan of Arc. It's never Joan Van Ark.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's exasperated correction implies this happens repeatedly
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Grandpa's confusion and Lisa's frustration. The 'never' suggests ongoing pattern.

jokes.text: This one takes place in a make-believe kingdom called France.
jokes.setup: Introduction to Joan of Arc story
jokes.punchline: Homer treats real country as fictional
jokes.explanation: Homer's American parochialism treating France as fantasy. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: The French were fighting the English in the Hundred Years' War, which was then called Operation Speedy Resolution.
jokes.punchline: War name ironically suggests it would be quick
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic military naming convention. Sharp commentary on optimistic war planning.

jokes.text: She's talking to her invisible friend. God.
jokes.punchline: Dismisses religious experience as imaginary friend
jokes.explanation: Sharp, economical commentary on religious experience from child's perspective. Well-delivered skepticism.

jokes.text: I have chosen you to lead the French army to victory over the English invaders. But I'm just a little girl. I know. I have three eyes. Now get cracking.
jokes.setup: God calling Joan to military leadership
jokes.punchline: Dismisses her concerns while mentioning his alien appearance
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist take on divine calling. Three-eyed God reference adds Simpsons-specific flavor.

jokes.text: Victory? We're French! We don't even have a word for it!
jokes.setup: Joan mentions leading France to victory
jokes.punchline: Stereotypical French military incompetence taken to extreme
jokes.explanation: Classic stereotype joke with good structure. Highly quotable but relies on familiar trope.

jokes.text: Joan, give me your dessert. That's just you, Bart. Joan, give me your dessert! Yes, sir.
jokes.setup: Bart tries to impersonate God's voice
jokes.punchline: Joan correctly identifies him then obeys a deeper voice anyway
jokes.explanation: Good sibling dynamic with religious gullibility. Shows Joan's literal-mindedness about divine commands.

jokes.text: That's going straight to my five thighs.
jokes.punchline: Bart has abnormally many thighs
jokes.explanation: Random physical abnormality gag. Economical but not particularly meaningful.

jokes.text: Huh? No, un. You know, French for 'one.' Well, you keep switching back between French and English. Just fire the damn thing.
jokes.punchline: Meta-commentary on the show's language inconsistency
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor about the show's linguistic shortcuts. Characters acknowledge the translation convention.

jokes.text: My fault this time. I didn't tuck in my legs.
jokes.punchline: Soldier takes personal responsibility for equipment malfunction
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy with good deadpan delivery. Soldier's self-blame is appropriately absurd.

jokes.text: With modern ideas, like putting bigger, harder people in the catapult. Or, how about rocks?
jokes.punchline: Escalates to using people as ammunition before suggesting the obvious
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure - goes from bad to obvious. Shows the backwards military thinking.

jokes.text: Shouldn't we help her? Yeah, right behind you, Lou.
jokes.punchline: Classic Lou and Eddie cowardice
jokes.explanation: Standard Lou and Eddie cowardice. Very character-consistent but predictable.

jokes.text: Hello, hello, hello. What's all this then? It's a little bird with a knife, isn't it? What a thing to happen!
jokes.setup: British officer encounters Joan in battle
jokes.punchline: Stereotypical British understatement about armed child warrior
jokes.explanation: Good British stereotype with understated reaction to violence. Patronizing tone works well.

jokes.text: You are the true dauphin. You had this impostor try to fool me to see if I was truly sent by God.
jokes.punchline: Shows divine insight by recognizing obvious test
jokes.explanation: Historical reference with Lisa's intelligence shining through. Somewhat dry but accurate to character.

jokes.text: Bravo, Joan of Arc. You are as brainsome as you are toothsome.
jokes.punchline: Archaic language praising both intelligence and attractiveness
jokes.explanation: Period-appropriate language with mild sexual undertones. Archaic word choice adds flavor.

jokes.text: Vibrate for me, footstool.
jokes.punchline: His job is being a vibrating footstool
jokes.explanation: Absurd degrading job for corrupt official. Economy and strangeness work well.

jokes.text: Uh, God says we should just be friends.
jokes.punchline: Joan uses divine authority to friend-zone him
jokes.explanation: Excellent modern dating language applied to historical/religious context. Perfect Lisa character moment.

jokes.text: I wouldn't say King Milhouse is a loser, but that's the 12th girl he's struck out with this week!
jokes.punchline: Absurdly high rejection rate in short time period
jokes.explanation: Good Milhouse characterization as perpetual loser. Escalation makes the rejection seem routine.

jokes.text: Their concept of individual rights could undermine the power of our beloved tyrants!
jokes.punchline: Explicitly supporting tyranny over freedom
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire about people supporting systems that oppress them. Good historical irony.

jokes.text: I want my mommy! I captured a wee girl! I'm the greatest hero in English history!
jokes.punchline: Soldier celebrates capturing a crying child as heroic achievement
jokes.explanation: Good ironic contrast between Joan's childish reaction and soldier's inflated pride.

jokes.text: Wait a minute, you two-timing spot of light. You told me to lead the English to victory!
jokes.punchline: Judge accuses God of playing both sides
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist concept of God hedging his bets in war. 'Two-timing spot of light' is particularly good.

jokes.text: Well, I never thought the two of you would be in the same room, actually. This is a little embarrassing. Goodbye now.
jokes.punchline: Awkward divine exit from embarrassing situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect follow-up showing God as bumbling rather than omniscient. Great awkward comedy.

jokes.text: Burn this guy! He lost a good bucket!
jokes.punchline: Immediately shifts focus to burning someone for property damage
jokes.explanation: Good mob mentality joke showing how quickly crowds redirect their anger.

jokes.text: Huh. Getting kind of hot around here.
jokes.setup: Joan being burned at the stake
jokes.punchline: Massive understatement about being on fire
jokes.explanation: Masterful deadpan understatement about death by fire. Perfect economy and dark humor timing.

jokes.text: Just then, Sir Lancelot rode up on a white horse and saved Joan of Arc. They got married and lived in a spaceship. The end.
jokes.setup: Homer improvises happy ending for disturbing story
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd elements culminating in space travel
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer parenting - lies to avoid uncomfortable topics with escalating absurdity.

jokes.text: Well, that's easier to chew than that Bambi video.
jokes.punchline: Lisa compares it favorably to other traumatic children's media
jokes.explanation: Good reference to Disney's reputation for traumatizing children. Lisa's pragmatic acceptance works.

jokes.text: Steven Bochco could kick Shakespeare's ass!
jokes.punchline: Absurd comparison of TV producer to greatest playwright
jokes.explanation: Good cultural reference showing Bart's pop culture priorities over high culture.

jokes.text: Cool. Does he get to marry his mom? I don't know, but that would be hot.
jokes.punchline: Immediate jump to incestuous speculation that Homer endorses
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about inappropriate family dynamics. Character-appropriate but borderline tasteless.

jokes.text: Looks like you've returned from the buffet. Why, you little... D'oh!
jokes.punchline: Comments on ghost's weight gain, triggers Homer's signature response
jokes.explanation: Perfect character integration - Homer's weight sensitivity persists even as ghost. Classic catchphrase use.

jokes.text: Yeah, that was quite a weekend.
jokes.setup: Learning about husband's murder and quick remarriage
jokes.punchline: Casual dismissal of traumatic events as busy weekend
jokes.explanation: Brilliant understatement treating murder/remarriage as routine weekend activities. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: It's cold outside. You'll need a sweater. A sweater!
jokes.punchline: Repeats sweater advice with ghostly drama
jokes.explanation: Ghost more concerned with son's comfort than revenge mission. Good parental priority comedy.

jokes.text: And if your idea of a first date is burning down her village, you just might be a Viking!
jokes.punchline: Medieval/Viking version of 'you might be a redneck'
jokes.explanation: Good parody of popular comedy format adapted to historical setting. Recognizable structure.

jokes.text: Note to self. Kill that kid.
jokes.punchline: Treats murderous intent as casual reminder note
jokes.explanation: Perfect casual treatment of murder as administrative task. Very economical delivery.

jokes.text: Hey, do you have diarrhea? I have diarrhea. Sit down!
jokes.punchline: Medical oversharing from audience, Claudius's frustrated response
jokes.explanation: Typical cringe comedy with bodily function humor. Works for the awkward performance setting.

jokes.text: I didn't use that much poi, son, at the royal luau.
jokes.setup: Claudius accidentally confessing to poison murder
jokes.punchline: Desperately tries to recontextualize 'poison' as Hawaiian food
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay escape attempt. Good panic response showing quick thinking under pressure.

jokes.text: Well nobody out-crazies Ophelia. Hey, nonny nonny, with a hoo and a haw, and a nonny nonny hey!
jokes.punchline: Ophelia responds with competitive madness using nonsense words
jokes.explanation: Good competitive dynamic with period-appropriate madness language. Lisa's overachieving nature shows.

jokes.text: I hide behind curtains 'cause I have a fear of getting stabbed.
jokes.punchline: His hiding strategy directly causes his feared outcome
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic setup-payoff. Wiggum's incompetent logic leads directly to the outcome he fears.

jokes.text: I need you to avenge my death. I like revenging!
jokes.punchline: Ralph's innocent enthusiasm for violence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph characterization - childlike enthusiasm for inappropriate concepts. Very true to character.

jokes.text: If Hamlet touches either of us, he's dead. Booyah!
jokes.punchline: Modern slang celebration in Shakespeare setting
jokes.explanation: Simple anachronism joke. Character-appropriate enthusiasm but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Boy, did I bet on the wrong horse.
jokes.setup: Ralph easily defeats Hamlet in practice
jokes.punchline: Claudius realizes his assassination plan is unnecessary
jokes.explanation: Good understatement about overcomplicating assassination. Moe's practical criminal thinking shows.

jokes.text: You sure you don't want a nice piece of fish, or to finger the drapes a little?
jokes.punchline: Claudius desperately offers poisoned alternatives
jokes.explanation: Panic-driven offer of previously mentioned death traps. Shows Claudius's desperation well.

jokes.text: Remember me as a peacemaker.
jokes.punchline: Murderer wants to be remembered for non-violence
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic last words from a murderer. Good economy and character-appropriate self-deception.

jokes.text: No way I'm cleaning up this mess.
jokes.punchline: Hamlet's practical concern about cleanup duty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart response to tragedy - immediate concern for personal inconvenience. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Son, it's not only a great play, but also became a great movie called Ghostbusters.
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong movie reference
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer cultural confusion. Ghost connection makes it almost logical but completely wrong.

tmdb_overview: When Homer gets a notice from the library that he has a book of classic tales that is years overdue, he finds it on the shelf and reads three stories: The Odyssey (where Homer and his bar buddies try to get home after fighting the Trojans), Joan of Arc (where Lisa leads the French against the English with the help of God), and Hamlet (where Bart tries to kill Moe after Moe kills Homer in order to marry Marge).
